# **Closed-Loop Training Methodologies for LLM-Based Autonomous Trading Agents: A Systems Architecture Report**

## **The Architectural Imperative for Closed-Loop Evolution**

The advancement of large language model (LLM) based autonomous agents requires moving beyond static system prompts and isolated execution environments into dynamic, closed-loop architectures. When an autonomous trading agent possesses disparate advanced systems—such as hierarchical memory, Brier score calibration, cumulative sum (CUSUM) edge decay detection, and high-fidelity sandbox simulations—the lack of an automated feedback pipeline creates a state of arrested development. Without automated integration, the agent generates exhaustive operational data but fails to achieve autonomous self-improvement.  
The transition from a static execution state to a fully autonomous, self-improving pipeline necessitates a formalized architecture that translates episodic market interactions into semantic knowledge, optimizes the agent's core prompt parameters via textual backpropagation, and rigorously validates these changes within a simulated environment. This report establishes the optimal end-to-end framework to connect a robust Rust-based algorithmic trading infrastructure into a unified training loop. The primary objectives are to prioritize computational velocity, strictly adhere to decision latency constraints, leverage the vast underlying episodic datasets, and prevent catastrophic prompt degradation during automated mutation cycles.  
Operating an LLM like MIMO v2.5 Pro via OpenGateway within a multi-threaded Rust environment provides exceptional concurrency advantages but imposes strict boundaries on context window utilization and non-deterministic response handling. Because the same prompt can yield different trading actions under identical market scenarios, singular evaluations are statistically invalid. Therefore, the integration of textual gradient optimization frameworks alongside advanced multi-run statistical evaluation mechanisms forms the bedrock of the continuous learning pipeline.

## **The Prioritized Step-by-Step Training Pipeline Architecture**

To connect the sandbox, memory, and prompt evolution mechanisms without exceeding the strict 500ms decision latency budget required during live trading, the architecture must completely decouple the inference pathway from the asynchronous learning pathway. Utilizing the existing Rust and tokio concurrency model, the closed-loop training architecture is formulated into a non-blocking cyclic pipeline that operates in the background, communicating with the live agent strictly through atomic database updates and read-write locks (Arc\<RwLock\<SharedEngineData\>\>).  
The optimal integration pathway follows a precisely scheduled, five-phase loop designed to be executable as a standalone command-line interface command (e.g., savant-cli pipeline \--execute full-loop) or run continuously via a background daemon.

| Pipeline Phase | Target Module | Execution Trigger | Primary Objective | Output Artifact |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **1\. Episodic Ingestion** | memory/episodic.rs | Every closed trade | Capture execution parameters, market context, and continuously update rolling calibration metrics. | SQLite WAL updates, CUSUM Edge Shift Alerts |
| **2\. Semantic Extraction** | memory/semantic.rs | Daily (Cron) or after 10 closed trades | Convert raw episodic string ledgers into statistically significant multi-variable probability matrices. | semantic\_patterns table population |
| **3\. Heuristic Reflexion** | memory/replay.rs | Weekly (Weekend) or Drawdown \> 5% | Execute self-reflection routines on high-conviction failures to extract generalized trading principles. | experience\_replay\_lessons table updates |
| **4\. Parameter Mutation** | sandbox/feedback.rs | CUSUM NegativeShift Alert or Weekly | Apply Genetic-Pareto (GEPA) textual differentiation to evolve system instructions based on extracted lessons. | Candidate SOUL\_v\_Next.md variants |
| **5\. Sandbox Validation** | sandbox/harness.rs | Post-Mutation | Forward-simulate mutated variants against Hawkes process limit order books utilizing sequential testing. | Validated production SOUL.md, Evaluation Reports |

This asynchronous structure ensures that the computationally heavy operations—such as executing 50 parallelized sandbox runs or analyzing thousands of database rows for semantic pattern extraction—never interfere with the execution thread responsible for polling the 5-minute Kraken candle data. When the pipeline completes Phase 5, the updated SOUL.md and modified semantic contexts are seamlessly hot-swapped into the SharedEngineData struct.

## **Semantic Extraction: Translating Episodic Traces into Actionable Intelligence**

The foundational step in the memory-driven learning loop requires moving data from the unstructured agent\_episodes table into the structured, actionable semantic\_patterns table. Currently, episodic memory captures exhaustive execution data, including regime classifications, specific knowledge units utilized, thesis summaries, and Brier calibration states. Extracting patterns requires a scheduled background process that queries this episodic ledger to calculate conditional probabilities and win rates across multi-dimensional feature spaces.

### **SQL Paradigms for Automated Pattern Population**

The population of the semantic\_patterns table cannot rely on real-time queries during the 500ms decision window; the computation must be pre-calculated. The extraction pipeline, operating via the sqlx SQLite integration, executes automated multi-variable grouped aggregations. To extract meaningful knowledge, the database queries must identify conditions under which the agent possesses a distinct statistical edge or a pronounced vulnerability.  
The most critical pattern categories to extract include:

1. **Regime and Session Matrices:** Performance variations across different macro environments.  
2. **Conviction Calibration:** Win rates stratified by internal confidence scoring versus actual Brier score penalty contexts.  
3. **Knowledge Unit Efficacy:** Tracking which dynamically injected JSON knowledge units correlate with Tier 2 R:R successes.

The following Rust sqlx query structure demonstrates the methodology for extracting the regime and session matrix, ensuring that only statistically significant patterns are promoted to semantic memory:

SQL  
INSERT INTO semantic\_patterns (  
    category, condition\_value, sample\_size, win\_rate, avg\_pnl, avg\_rr, is\_valid, confidence\_penalty  
)  
SELECT   
    'REGIME\_SESSION\_MATRIX' as category,  
    market\_regime || '\_' || trading\_session as condition\_value,  
    COUNT(episode\_id) as sample\_size,  
    SUM(CASE WHEN is\_win \= 1 THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) \* 1.0 / COUNT(episode\_id) as win\_rate,  
    AVG(pnl\_percentage) as avg\_pnl,  
    AVG(achieved\_rr) as avg\_rr,  
    1 as is\_valid,  
    0.0 as confidence\_penalty  
FROM agent\_episodes  
WHERE status \= 'CLOSED'   
GROUP BY market\_regime, trading\_session  
HAVING sample\_size \>= 30;

To ensure statistical validity and prevent the agent from anchoring to market noise, the extraction pipeline rigorously enforces a minimum sample size constraint. Patterns are only promoted to the semantic layer if the sample size ![][image1] for macro-patterns, or ![][image2] for highly specific micro-patterns (e.g., funding rate ranges combined with RSI divergences). Furthermore, pattern decay is managed by implementing a rolling 90-day lookback window in the SQL WHERE clause, ensuring that an edge extracted in January naturally decays and is overwritten if it ceases to function by March.

### **Prompt Engineering for Memory Context Injection**

Once patterns are successfully populated in the semantic database, they must be formatted for LLM consumption. The 1M token context window of MIMO v2.5 Pro allows for extensive inclusion, but the available 8K token budget specifically allocated for knowledge injection necessitates extreme efficiency. Providing the agent with raw statistical tables or unformatted SQL dumps typically results in reasoning degradation and hallucination.  
The optimal injection format relies on declarative, probabilistically grounded statements. The dynamic memory context injected into the user message should prioritize patterns exhibiting the highest deviation from the agent's baseline performance. This contextual injection is formatted as a structured JSON or clear Markdown sub-section appended directly after the market data stream.  
An optimized prompt template for dynamic memory injection follows this structural logic:

### **Dynamic Memory Context**

Based on your historical episodic ledger, the following statistically significant patterns apply to the current market condition:

* **Strategic Edge:** Under regimes during the \[Asian\] session, LONG setups targeting a minimum of 1.5 R:R have historically yielded a 68% win rate (Sample Size: 42).  
* **Vulnerability Warning:** Recent CUSUM edge decay analysis indicates a persistent performance shift (NegativeShift) in mean-reversion trades on when ADX \> 25\. Profit factor has declined from 1.4 to 0.8.  
* **Calibration State:** Your current rolling Brier Score is 0.18. You are operating within normal calibration parameters; HIGH conviction sizing is authorized.

This probabilistic phrasing naturally aligns with the LLM's internal mechanisms, allowing the agent to explicitly reference these historical success rates in its reasoning trace and dynamically adjust its confidence scoring based on its own empirical history. The agent is explicitly shown its Brier score and CUSUM status at decision time to induce self-correction, provided the phrasing remains analytical rather than punitive.

## **Experience Replay and Heuristic Reflexion Optimization**

The experience replay system, designed to query HIGH conviction losses and missed opportunities, serves as the primary engine for verbal reinforcement learning within the architecture. While episodic memory captures the raw numeric data, experience replay attempts to capture the higher-order reasoning failures that led to misallocated capital. The application of self-reflection allows autonomous agents to dynamically adapt to complex failures by generating corrective heuristics.1

### **Reflexion Methodology and Teacher LLMs**

Experience replay operates optimally as an intensive, asynchronous weekend retrospective. Rather than forcing the operational trading agent to critique itself during active sessions, the architecture employs a secondary, dedicated LLM call—often utilizing maximum reasoning temperature parameters—to act as the "Teacher Agent."  
The Teacher Agent is presented with the complete, unaltered episodic trajectory of a failed HIGH conviction trade. This trace includes the original order book state, the synthesized thesis, the executed parameters, and the ultimate market outcome. Employing frameworks derived from advanced Reflexion methodologies, the agent generates a deep verbal critique of the discrepancy between its expected outcome and the empirical reality.3  
However, unrestricted self-reflection in LLMs often results in overly verbose, overly specific, or entirely hallucinated constraints. A trade lost due to a sudden macroeconomic catalyst might result in the agent incorrectly generating a rule to never trade that specific asset again. Therefore, the output of the replay cycle must be heavily constrained. The system enforces a rule that the final output must be synthesized into a single-sentence heuristic lesson, stored securely in the experience\_replay\_lessons table.

### **Mitigating Context Dilution and Measuring Efficacy**

A common failure mode in self-improving agents is the endless accumulation of lessons, which eventually dilutes the system prompt to the point of paralysis. To maximize impact while mitigating context dilution, a maximum of three to five highly relevant replay lessons are retrieved and injected per live decision cycle.  
Retrieval utilizes a multi-factor matching system, comparing the current live market context against the precise market context prevailing when the lesson was initially generated. If a lesson is injected and the subsequent trade is successful (achieving a Tier 2 score ![][image3]), its applied\_count metric increments.  
Crucially, the system must measure if a lesson actually improves future decisions. A lifecycle management protocol is applied to the replay table: if a specific heuristic lesson is injected into the prompt three separate times, and all three resulting trades result in compliance failures or negative PnL, the lesson is autonomously pruned from the database. This ensures the system prompt remains a lean, highly optimized compilation of genuinely effective wisdom.

## **Prompt Evolution via Textual Differentiation and GEPA**

The identity of the Savant agent is fundamentally encoded within the 879-line SOUL.md document. The current Rust infrastructure contains modules that detect failures and propose mutations, but it severely lacks an automated execution mechanism to safely apply them. Manually reviewing and updating system prompts is a developmental anti-pattern that fails to scale; instead, the system must utilize automatic differentiation and backpropagation through textual feedback to achieve continuous optimization.5

### **The Genetic-Pareto (GEPA) Optimization Architecture**

To safely and continuously mutate SOUL.md, the architecture implements a Genetic-Pareto (GEPA) optimization loop.8 Traditional numerical optimization detects that a parameter set failed, but textual gradient frameworks allow the system to reflect on execution traces to diagnose precisely why the failure occurred.9 GEPA attempts to find the optimal system prompt by repeatedly cycling through a genetic optimization process: scoring candidates across multiple axes, selecting via Pareto efficiency criteria, and mutating to produce novel candidate prompts.10  
The SOUL.md document must first be rigorously segmented to prevent the LLM from destroying critical operational frameworks. Sections dictating JSON schema outputs, strict risk management boundaries, and tool utilization constraints are classified as immutable invariants. Sections containing tactical execution heuristics, indicator weightings, and specific market condition interpretations are classified as mutable nodes.  
The GEPA implementation follows a highly structured operational sequence:

1. **Trace Analysis:** The feedback.rs module analyzes Tier 2 (Risk:Reward) and Tier 3 (Reasoning Quality) grading failures generated during sandbox simulations.  
2. **Gradient Generation:** The framework evaluates the error trace and generates a textual gradient. This gradient functions similarly to a backpropagation update step—it provides specific natural language instructions on how to alter a mutable node in SOUL.md to correct the failure without disrupting concurrent operational systems.5  
3. **Mutation & Crossover:** The textual gradient is applied to the current production prompt to create SOUL\_v\_Next. The GEPA system inherently utilizes crossover techniques, blending successful prompt variants from different branches of the evolutionary tree to construct the new candidate.8  
4. **Sandbox Validation:** The candidate prompt is immediately subjected to a parallelized execution run across the 50-scenario sandbox.

### **A/B Testing, Version Control, and Automated Rollback**

Promoting a mutated SOUL.md to live trading requires rigorous statistical validation. The system utilizes an ![][image4]\-trimmed mean evaluation (20% trim) across 3 to 5 multi-run sandbox executions to account for the inherent non-determinism of the LLM.  
If SOUL\_v\_Next achieves a statistically significant improvement in the aggregate Weighted Sandbox Aggregate (WSA) without triggering a regression alert in critical compliance categories, the mutation is staged for promotion. The soul\_versions SQLite table assigns a unique cryptographic hash to the new document, mapping it permanently to all subsequent episodic memory entries it generates in live trading. This guarantees complete auditability, allowing human operators to track precisely which prompt version produced which financial results.  
If the sandbox score drops after a mutation, or if the check\_regression() function triggers an alert due to a score falling below a predefined historical rolling average, the GEPA optimizer executes an automated rollback. The variant is discarded, the specific textual gradient is stored in memory as a known "failed evolutionary path," and the system reverts to the last stable version, attempting a divergent evolutionary branch on the next cycle. Human-in-the-loop approvals are reserved strictly for mutations that attempt to alter any structural risk limits, while tactical mutations are fully automated.

## **Mitigating Prompt Anti-Patterns and Over-Constraining**

As the agent learns from continuous feedback loops, there is an inherent systemic risk of the prompt accumulating excessive constraints. When an LLM is continuously fed negative feedback regarding edge cases, it frequently develops a hyper-conservative operational bias. Injecting dozens of highly specific "Do Not Trade" conditions into the prompt creates prompt stacking, a documented anti-pattern that over-complicates instructions, fragments the LLM's attention mechanism, and ultimately scales poorly due to limited instruction-following capabilities across long contexts.14

### **Consolidating Negative Knowledge**

To prevent the agent from freezing into an overly conservative state, anti-patterns detected within the episodic memory must be heavily abstracted before injection. Rather than appending discrete, standalone rules for every failed condition, the system dynamically refactors these constraints.  
Industry research on LLM prompt safety engineering indicates that long lists of specific edge cases reliably compress into a smaller number of principled statements when supported by highly specific illustrative examples.15 For instance, rather than the SOUL.md Action Triggers accumulating raw rules such as prohibiting trades during high funding rates, high RSI values, and Asian sessions individually, the GEPA optimizer refactors these anti-patterns into a unified guiding principle:

* **Refactored Principle:** "Strictly avoid mean-reversion entries during periods demonstrating synchronized directional exhaustion and structural liquidity constraints."

This higher-level principle is then supported by three highly specific, few-shot JSON examples embedded within the prompt, demonstrating a decisive Hold action under those exact conditions. This mechanism effectively delegates the constraint handling to the LLM's pattern matching capabilities rather than forcing strict rule-based logic. It achieves the required safety and anti-pattern avoidance while drastically reducing token overhead, recovering attention span, and preventing the instructional pollution that causes agents to cease trading entirely.15

## **Dynamic Knowledge Unit Lifecycle Management**

The agent currently selects from a massive static repository of 2,959 distinct knowledge units distributed across 10 JSON files, utilizing a Maximum Marginal Relevance (MMR) algorithm based on market conditions. While effective for basic semantic retrieval, this static repository ignores the empirical utility of the information provided in actual live trading. The system requires an autonomous agentic memory architecture that enables the dynamic evolution, pruning, and promotion of these knowledge units based directly on real-world financial utility.17

### **Empirical Utility Scoring Algorithms**

Every knowledge unit injected into the 8K token budget during a decision cycle is tracked through the episodic memory framework. To bridge the gap between retrieval and performance, an empirical utility score (![][image5]) is permanently assigned to each unit within the static files.  
The lifecycle algorithm continuously adjusts ![][image5] using a formalized reinforcement learning paradigm. If a knowledge unit is injected and the resulting live trade successfully achieves a Tier 2 R:R grade ![][image3], the unit's utility score is incremented by a promotion factor (![][image4]). Conversely, if the unit is injected and the trade results in a loss or a critical Tier 1 compliance failure, the score is decremented by a steep penalty factor (![][image6]).  
When the MMR algorithm subsequently executes to select knowledge for the prompt, the base semantic similarity score is multiplied by a logarithmic function of the utility score:  
![][image7]  
Consequently, knowledge units that consistently correlate with highly profitable market execution are organically promoted to the highest retrieval priority. In contrast, units containing obsolete strategies or data that correlate with losing trades are progressively suppressed.

### **Automated Pruning and Organic Generation**

Knowledge units possessing a utility score that falls below a critical minimum threshold are automatically flagged for archival or aggressive pruning, maintaining the efficiency of the vector search space.18 Furthermore, the system autonomously generates novel knowledge units derived from its own experience. When the experience replay system synthesizes a highly effective heuristic lesson that survives multiple evaluation loops and demonstrates persistent profitability, the pipeline formalizes that lesson. It is automatically converted into a structured JSON knowledge unit, tagged with the appropriate market context vectors, and permanently added to the repository, entirely bypassing the need for manual human data entry.

## **Brier Score Calibration and The Cold-Start Strategy**

The agent's risk management framework relies heavily on its internal conviction levels (HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW), which dictate position sizing, leverage, and execution aggressiveness. However, generative LLMs are notoriously prone to severe miscalibration, frequently expressing high confidence in statistically improbable outcomes. The integration of the Brier score calibration mechanism bridges the critical gap between the agent's subjective conviction and objective empirical probability, linking mathematical forecasting accuracy directly to live trading behavior.20

### **Mathematical Formulation of the Feedback Loop**

The Brier score (![][image8]) measures the mean squared error between the model's predicted probabilities (![][image9]) and the binary market outcomes (![][image10]), where 1 represents a successful trade.  
![][image11]  
A Brier score of 0.0 indicates perfect oracle calibration, while a score of 0.25 represents an agent performing indistinguishably from random chance, and scores above 0.25 indicate negative predictive validity.21 The existing infrastructure effectively maps text-based conviction levels to numerical probabilities (HIGH=0.75, MEDIUM=0.50, LOW=0.25).  
To effectively close the loop, the system leverages a daily recalculated rolling Brier score spanning the most recent 50 trades. This metric dynamically constrains the agent's permitted conviction outputs during live trading. If the rolling Brier score deteriorates beyond ![][image12], indicating severe systemic overconfidence, the calibration pipeline enforces an automatic hard cap. Regardless of the agent's internal reasoning or technical analysis, any output attempting to utilize HIGH conviction is systematically downgraded to MEDIUM or LOW, effectively minimizing capital risk until the agent's probabilistic forecasting reliability improves.20

### **Resolving the Bayesian Cold-Start Problem**

The existing system defaults to progressive confidence caps, restricting the agent exclusively to LOW conviction until a baseline of 25 trades is achieved. This creates a severe cold-start problem, crippling the agent's ability to capitalize on highly asymmetric opportunities early in its deployment lifecycle.  
To resolve this without assuming unacceptable risk, the architecture integrates Bayesian priors derived from the agent's rigorous sandbox performance. Because the agent continuously runs against the 50-scenario sandbox, it possesses an established simulated Brier score. If a newly mutated SOUL\_v\_Next achieves a highly calibrated synthetic Brier score (e.g., ![][image13]) during its multi-run evaluation, the live trading system initiates with this simulated metric acting as a mathematical prior. This immediately authorizes the use of MEDIUM or HIGH conviction. As live trades accumulate, the weighting of the sandbox prior logarithmically diminishes, allowing the empirical live data to seamlessly overtake the initial synthetic baseline by the 25th trade.

## **CUSUM Edge Decay and Dynamic Scenario Expansion**

A critical systemic flaw in standard algorithmic trading agents is their fundamental inability to adapt to subtle macro-regime shifts until significant capital drawdowns have already occurred. The implementation of Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) control charts for edge decay detection provides highly sensitive early warning systems. However, these mathematical alerts are useless unless they trigger concrete, targeted training actions within the closed loop.

### **CUSUM Activation Protocols**

The CUSUM statistic rigorously tracks cumulative deviations in achieved Risk:Reward from the target parameter (1.5 R:R).  
![][image14]  
Where ![][image15] is the target metric, ![][image16] is the baseline mean, and ![][image17] is the allowance value. When the continuous calculation triggers a NegativeShift alert within the semantic memory, it serves as the absolute catalyst for immediate, out-of-cycle GEPA optimization.  
If edge decay is explicitly detected on a specific asset or during a specific regime (e.g., Ethereum during range-bound weekend sessions), the closed-loop pipeline automatically queries the episodic memory for all recent failures occurring within that exact cross-section. These specific failures are then systematically transmitted to the synthetic sandbox generator to create highly targeted adversarial scenarios designed to break the current SOUL.md iteration.

### **Expanding Scenarios via Hawkes Process Simulation**

Static libraries containing only 50 hand-curated scenarios are woefully insufficient for ensuring long-term robustness. To organically expand the sandbox without succumbing to scenario bloat, the specific live failures that trigger CUSUM alerts must be accurately converted into replayable, forward-simulated environments.  
The sandbox's order book simulator (lob.rs) utilizes a weakly consistent, multivariate Hawkes process, allowing for the incredibly realistic recreation of volatile market microstructure and dynamic liquidity events.24 The Hawkes process accurately models limit order book arrivals as a self-exciting point process, where the intensity ![][image18] of incoming order arrivals is heavily conditioned on the history of past market events.26  
![][image19]  
By feeding the synthetic generator the exact historical sequence of Kraken trades, volume profiles, and volatility metrics that resulted in the live failure, the sandbox dynamically reconstructs the event-based limit order book.27 This pipeline effectively transforms a unique, isolated live trading loss into a permanent, highly rigorous sandbox scenario. The GEPA optimizer is subsequently unleashed upon this newly generated scenario, forcing the LLM to evolve a prompt mutation that successfully navigates the exact microstructural market condition that previously caused edge decay. To prevent unchecked library bloat, these personalized scenarios are tracked; if the agent passes the scenario perfectly in five consecutive subsequent evaluation runs, the scenario is archived.

## **Training Velocity and Curriculum Learning Optimization**

Executing the full 50-scenario evaluation library after every minor mutation would drastically exceed computational budgets and violate the 2-hour maximum full training cycle constraint. The system's training velocity must be aggressively optimized through targeted curriculum learning and statistical sampling efficiencies.

### **Multi-Tiered Curriculum Delivery**

Instead of executing all 50 scenarios sequentially, the sandbox harness implements a dynamic, adaptive curriculum. The scenarios are categorized into 11 distinct groups (e.g., Trend Bull, Trend Bear, Microstructure, Sentiment/On-Chain). The agent's historical performance matrix dictates the targeted sampling frequency.  
If the agent scores an exceptional 0.90 on Trend Bull scenarios but consistently fails, scoring 0.40, on Extended On-Chain environments, the validation harness heavily skews the curriculum weighting during the next GEPA iteration. Targeted remediation principles dictate that evaluating the agent against 15 highly complex variants of an On-Chain scenario utilizing the GARCH(1,1) generator yields faster parameter convergence and performance improvement than running the entire broad 50-scenario suite.  
Furthermore, adversarial training is integrated by isolating the precise conditions of historical Tier 1 (Compliance) failures. When a prompt mutation is initially proposed, it is evaluated against an adversarial "Gauntlet" curriculum—a subset of 5 heavily volatile, microstructure-focused scenarios specifically engineered to induce compliance failures. If the mutation fails the Gauntlet, it is instantaneously discarded, saving the vast computational token expense of a full validation run.

### **Statistical Significance in Non-Deterministic LLM Environments**

Because the LLM generation process is inherently non-deterministic—capable of generating entirely different reasoning pathways and executing different trades for identical synthetic scenarios—singular sandbox executions are mathematically invalid for proving the efficacy of a prompt mutation. The system utilizes an ![][image4]\-trimmed mean (20% trim) across 3 to 5 parallel runs to eliminate statistical outliers caused by LLM hallucination.  
To dramatically increase training velocity without compromising this statistical significance, the sandbox harness utilizes sequential testing algorithms. After the completion of the first two parallel runs, the evaluation engine calculates the maximum possible theoretical score the candidate could achieve. If the candidate's initial performance is so poor that it remains mathematically incapable of surpassing the current production baseline—even if the remaining 3 runs achieve perfect 1.0 scores—the multi-run evaluation is immediately short-circuited and terminated early. This optimization drastically reduces token expenditure and API execution time, easily keeping the entire training cycle well beneath the 2-hour constraint.

## **Measurement Frameworks and Overfitting Prevention**

A self-improving algorithmic system requires rigorous measurement frameworks to distinguish genuine alpha generation and logic enhancement from mere sandbox overfitting. If the GEPA loop strictly optimizes for the exact parameters of the 50 known static scenarios, the agent will simply memorize the synthetic Hawkes order book patterns, resulting in catastrophic failure when deployed into live, unseen market conditions.

### **Primary Evaluation Metrics and Target Benchmarks**

The primary singular metric for determining training effectiveness is the **Weighted Sandbox Aggregate (WSA)**. The WSA is a composite scoring mechanism designed to heavily prioritize operational consistency and reasoning over peak episodic performance.

* **WSA Calculation:** (Tier 2 Average ![][image20] 0.4) \+ (Tier 3 Average ![][image20] 0.6)  
* **The Penalty Clause:** Any instance of a Tier 1 critical compliance failure (e.g., executing a trade with a missing stop loss or violating leverage constraints) applies an automatic, unmitigable ![][image21] penalty multiplier to the entire category's total WSA score.

An initial baseline WSA of 0.60 is acceptable for a newly deployed, uncalibrated agent. The target benchmark for the closed-loop system, after 10 autonomous training and mutation iterations, should approach 0.82. However, scores rapidly exceeding 0.90 typically indicate severe prompt overfitting and warrant immediate investigation by a human operator, as perfect scores across highly volatile synthetic environments are mathematically improbable without logic memorization. The expected training curve should demonstrate fast initial gains as glaring compliance and basic R:R logic errors are eliminated, followed by diminishing marginal returns as the GEPA optimizer struggles to extract edge from increasingly complex microstructure scenarios.

### **Held-Out Validation Sets and Live Correlation Tracking**

To explicitly combat the danger of overfitting, the entire scenario library is strictly partitioned. The GEPA textual optimizer only possesses evaluation access to a "Training Set" comprising 40 scenarios. The remaining 10 scenarios are securely isolated and serve as a blind "Validation Set." A mutated SOUL\_v\_Next candidate may only be promoted to production if its performance demonstrates statistically significant improvement on the Training Set while simultaneously maintaining or improving its score on the Held-Out Validation Set.  
If the aggregate score surges on the training set but violently regresses on the validation set, the model has definitively overfit the synthetic data. The regression\_detector.rs module actively monitors the rolling average of these validation scores. If the validation score drops by more than ![][image22] standard deviations against the historical baseline, an immediate alert is triggered, and the mutation is forcefully rolled back to the last known stable configuration.  
Ultimately, sandbox evaluation scores are fundamentally irrelevant if they fail to correlate with live trading Profit and Loss (PnL). The episodic memory ledger continuously executes Pearson correlation coefficients, comparing the agent's 7-day rolling live R:R achievement against its most recent WSA validation score. If this correlation begins to diverge—meaning sandbox scores steadily increase while live PnL simultaneously decreases—it indicates a severe structural drift between the synthetic GARCH/Hawkes generation models and actual live market microstructure. This divergence automatically triggers a system-wide recalibration of the simulator's internal drift, volatility, and order arrival intensity parameters, ensuring the training loop remains perfectly aligned with the empirical reality of the cryptocurrency markets.

## **Synthesis of the Closed-Loop Architecture**

The optimal architecture for achieving measurable, continuous improvement in an LLM-based autonomous trading agent requires the seamless orchestration of episodic memory extraction, natural language prompt mutation, rigorous statistical calibration, and high-fidelity microstructure simulation into a single, asynchronous pipeline.  
By strategically offloading computationally heavy operations—such as semantic SQL aggregations, experience replay generation, and GEPA optimization loops—to scheduled background tasks, the system preserves the critical sub-500ms decision latency required for live market execution. Utilizing textual gradients to autonomously evolve the agent's core identity constraints, and validating those structural changes against organically expanding, Hawkes-process-driven simulated limit order books, provides a verifiable mechanism for self-improvement.  
The application of continuous Brier score calibration to dynamically govern conviction levels, combined with the rigorous pruning of knowledge units based solely on empirical utility, guarantees that the agent remains resilient. Through this architecture, the agent transcends static prompt engineering, dynamically adapting to volatile market regime shifts and fulfilling the immense potential of fully autonomous algorithmic market interaction.

#### **Works cited**

1. Self-Reflection in LLM Agents: Effects on Problem-Solving Performance \- arXiv, accessed May 31, 2026, [https://arxiv.org/pdf/2405.06682](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2405.06682)

3. When Can LLMs Actually Correct Their Own Mistakes? A Critical Survey of Self-Correction of LLMs | Transactions of the Association for Computational Linguistics, accessed May 31, 2026, [https://direct.mit.edu/tacl/article/doi/10.1162/tacl\_a\_00713/125177/When-Can-LLMs-Actually-Correct-Their-Own-Mistakes](https://direct.mit.edu/tacl/article/doi/10.1162/tacl_a_00713/125177/When-Can-LLMs-Actually-Correct-Their-Own-Mistakes)  
4. Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning \- arXiv, accessed May 31, 2026, [https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.11366](https://arxiv.org/pdf/2303.11366)  
5. TextGrad: Automatic ''Differentiation'' via Text \-- using large language models to backpropagate textual gradients. Published in Nature. \- GitHub, accessed May 31, 2026, [https://github.com/zou-group/textgrad](https://github.com/zou-group/textgrad)  
6. TextGrad integration · langfuse · Discussion \#3478 \- GitHub, accessed May 31, 2026, [https://github.com/orgs/langfuse/discussions/3478](https://github.com/orgs/langfuse/discussions/3478)  
7. Auto-Differentiating Any LLM Workflow: A Farewell to Manual Prompting \- arXiv, accessed May 31, 2026, [https://arxiv.org/html/2501.16673v1](https://arxiv.org/html/2501.16673v1)  
8. Demystifying GEPA (Genetic-Pareto) Prompt Optimizer | by GUANGYUAN PIAO \- Medium, accessed May 31, 2026, [https://medium.com/@parklize/demystifying-gepa-genetic-pareto-prompt-optimizer-53db5081cdb2](https://medium.com/@parklize/demystifying-gepa-genetic-pareto-prompt-optimizer-53db5081cdb2)
